name: Cattail
habitat: Wetlands, marshes, slow-moving water, and muddy pond edges.
weight: Varies by stalk and root mass
behaviorTemperament: Stationary plant. Usually found in dense stands near water.
diet: Photosynthetic plant. Requires wet soil or shallow water.
activityPattern: Seasonal growth. Most visible during warm months.
bestTimeToFind: Spring through fall near wetlands and pond edges.
observationDifficulty: Low, but safe use depends on correct identification.
description: A wetland plant commonly found near ponds, marshes, and slow-moving water.
lore: A classic survival plant because it can signal water nearby and has multiple traditional field uses.
survivalUse: Can indicate nearby water. Some parts are traditionally used as emergency food when properly identified.
handlingWarning: Never eat wild plants unless identification is certain. Avoid plants growing in polluted water.
encounterAdvice: Use as a water indicator, but check the water source for pollution, fuel, algae blooms, or runoff.
firstAid: If a wild plant causes nausea, burning, swelling, rash, or breathing issues, stop exposure and seek medical help.
fieldNote: Never eat wild plants unless you are fully confident in the identification.

name: Boiling Water
route: Any camp with water, heat, and a safe container
habitat: Applies to streams, lakes, collected rainwater, and other field water sources.
behaviorTemperament: Requires heat, time, and a safe container.
activityPattern: Best performed before drinking or storing questionable water.
bestTimeToFind: Any time water must be made safer and fire or heat is available.
observationDifficulty: Low skill barrier, but requires supplies.
description: A basic method for making water safer by killing many harmful organisms.
lore: One of the oldest and most reliable emergency water safety methods when chemical contamination is not the main concern.
survivalUse: Useful when you have a fire-safe container and enough fuel.
handlingWarning: Boiling water can still be unsafe if contaminated by fuel, chemicals, salt, or heavy metals.
encounterAdvice: Use when biological contamination is suspected and you have a clean, fire-safe container.
firstAid: Treat burns with cool clean water when available, protect the area, and seek medical care for serious burns.
fieldNote: Boiling does not remove chemicals, fuel, salt, or heavy metals.

name: Hypothermia
rarity: Common in cold or wet exposure
region: Cold, wet, windy, or high-altitude areas
route: Rainy trails, mountains, rivers, winter roads, exposed camps
habitat: Any environment where cold, wind, wet clothing, or exhaustion can drop body temperature.
behaviorTemperament: Progressive and easy to underestimate. Confusion can make self-rescue harder.
activityPattern: Most likely during cold, wet, windy, exhausted, or poorly sheltered conditions.
bestTimeToFind: Night, storms, winter travel, river crossings, and wet windy exposure.
observationDifficulty: Medium. Early signs can be subtle.
description: A dangerous drop in body temperature caused by cold, wind, wet clothing, or exposure.
lore: A quiet hazard that can worsen before the victim realizes how much danger they are in.
survivalUse: Recognizing it early helps you prioritize shelter, dry clothing, insulation, and warmth.
handlingWarning: Severe symptoms require emergency help. Do not ignore confusion, stumbling, or uncontrollable shivering.
encounterAdvice: Stop exposure early. Get dry, block wind, insulate from the ground, and warm the person gradually.
firstAid: Call emergency help for severe symptoms. Remove wet clothing when possible, insulate, protect from wind, and avoid rough handling.
fieldNote: Seek emergency medical help when symptoms are severe or worsening.

name: Windbreak Shelter
route: Open camps, exposed ridges, beaches, cold fields
habitat: Useful anywhere wind exposure increases heat loss or makes rest difficult.
behaviorTemperament: Passive structure. Effectiveness depends on placement and stability.
activityPattern: Most useful before rest, storms, cold nights, or wind exposure.
bestTimeToFind: Before sunset, before weather worsens, or immediately after identifying wind exposure.
observationDifficulty: Low. Placement quality matters more than complexity.
description: A simple barrier that reduces wind exposure using natural cover, gear, branches, or terrain.
lore: A low-tech field defense against exposure. Even a small wind reduction can make a camp more survivable.
survivalUse: Helps reduce heat loss and makes resting areas more survivable.
handlingWarning: Do not build under dead branches, unstable slopes, flood channels, or unsafe trees.
encounterAdvice: Build on safe ground with wind direction in mind. Prioritize stability, drainage, and overhead hazards.
firstAid: Not a medical treatment, but may help prevent worsening cold exposure.
signsOfPresence: Gear positioned as a wind block
fieldNote: Avoid unsafe trees, flood paths, unstable slopes, and areas under dead branches.

name: Bulbasaur
route: Shaded forest margins, old orchards, wet meadows, abandoned farms, and protected research preserves
habitat: Damp woodland floors with filtered sunlight, rich soil, leaf litter, moss beds, and reliable water
behaviorTemperament: Cautious, watchful, and usually non-aggressive unless cornered or separated from its nest patch. Young Bulbasaur freeze before fleeing and may release powder if handled roughly.
diet: Sunlight, mineral-rich water, soft shoots, berries, fungi, insects, and decomposing leaf matter absorbed through the bulb and mouth.
activityPattern: Mostly diurnal with crepuscular feeding. Rests during hard midday sun and becomes still during drought.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning after rain, near mossy logs or shaded water runoffs where the bulb can photosynthesize without drying out.
observationDifficulty: Moderate. The bulb resembles young vegetation and the body blends into fern shadows.
conservationStatus: Protected starter-class species in most managed regions
populationStatus: Locally stable in preserves, uncommon outside guarded habitat
description: A young quadrupedal seed Pokemon carrying a living plant bulb fused into its back. In a survival setting it is useful but unsafe to treat like a normal animal.
lore: Old forest accounts describe Bulbasaur appearing after battlefields and burned farms, rooting in soil enriched by ash and bone. Villages once treated them as gentle grave-garden guardians.
survivalUse: Can locate clean water, edible roots, damp shelter zones, and chemically damaged soil. Its bulb can shade a small wound but should not be harvested.
handlingWarning: Do not pull the bulb or vines. Do not place face near the bud. Powder exposure can inflame eyes, lungs, and skin.
encounterAdvice: Crouch low, keep hands visible, avoid fire, and let it smell your gear before moving closer. Back away if the bulb opens.
firstAid: Flush powder from eyes and skin with clean water. Treat vine bruising as soft-tissue injury. Seek urgent care for breathing difficulty or spreading rash.
companionSuitability: Good for patient handlers who can manage spores and habitat needs
fieldNote: If the bulb tracks your movement while the head stays still, the plant half has noticed you before the animal half has decided whether to run.
originStory: The darkest origin theory claims the bulb symbiosis began when a forest amphibian survived famine by accepting a parasitic seed that fed on its blood. Over generations, the parasite stopped killing the host and became a second body.

evolutionLine.name: Bulbasaur
evolutionLine.note: Seed-bearing juvenile. Handle as spore-capable even before the bulb opens.

evolutionLine.name: Ivysaur
evolutionLine.note: Flowering adolescent. Higher toxin and vine risk than Bulbasaur.

evolutionLine.name: Venusaur
evolutionLine.note: Large keystone adult. Treat groves as respiratory hazard zones.

mainLine.name: Bulbasaur
mainLine.note: Seed-bearing juvenile. Handle as spore-capable even before the bulb opens.

mainLine.name: Ivysaur
mainLine.note: Flowering adolescent. Higher toxin and vine risk than Bulbasaur.

mainLine.name: Venusaur
mainLine.note: Large keystone adult. Treat groves as respiratory hazard zones.

target.name: Mega Venusaur
target.note: Temporary empowered form with greater bulk and spore density. Not a normal wild life stage.

target.name: Gigantamax Venusaur
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Pollen and vine hazard should be treated as area-scale emergency.

domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated only under licensed starter programs
prey: Soft-bodied insects, small grubs, berries, fallen fruit, fungal nodules, and nutrient-rich plant matter
territoryRange: Usually 0.2 to 1 square mile around a water source and shaded feeding lane
lifeCycle: Hatches with a dormant seed-bulb, grows through photosynthesis and protein feeding, evolves into Ivysaur at level 16 when the bulb begins flowering.
containmentRequirements: Humid terrarium pen with live soil, drainage, filtered sunlight, non-toxic plants, and spore ventilation. Do not use bare concrete.
ecologicalRole: Seed disperser, insect regulator, soil aerator, and early warning species for herbicide contamination
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Bulb rot, fungal mites, pollen allergy, and soil-borne bacterial exposure are the main concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are laid in warm compost-like nests under leaves. Adults bury nest sites in loam and defend them with powder clouds rather than direct bites.
predators: Fearow, Arbok, larger fire-adapted predators, feral dogs, and humans seeking illegal starter specimens
emergencyResponse: For spore release, move upwind, rinse eyes for 15 minutes, remove contaminated clothing, and isolate the animal in a dim humid space.
legalStatus: Protected grass-poison species. Handling normally requires starter or botanical hazard certification.
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years before evolving or being displaced. Managed care: 8 to 12 years if evolution is delayed safely.
socialStructure: Small juvenile clusters near an older Ivysaur or Venusaur territory. Individuals recognize scent marks from their own line.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Elemental output is spore, vine, and solar stored energy.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Powder hazard usually 5 to 15 ft, farther in wind
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Short ground burst about 10 to 15 mph
fieldCapabilities.balance: Very stable due to low body and bulb counterweight

trainingRisk: Powder discharge, vine lash, accidental poisoning, and bulb infection if overtrained
physiology: Bulbasaur functions as a paired organism: an animal host and a vascular plant bulb linked by root-like tendrils. The bulb gathers sunlight, stores sugars, and can release irritant spores. The host supplies water, minerals, warmth, and mobility.

keyFacts.lifestyle: Diurnal, cautious, juvenile cluster or supervised starter
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years. Managed: 8 to 12 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually 1 to 4 juveniles near a family territory

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Trust is possible but humidity, diet, and stress control matter.
physicalCharacteristics: Stocky blue-green body, red eyes, blunt claws, wide mouth, sturdy legs, and a green dorsal bulb with dark mottling. Skin is smooth but tougher than it appears and often smells of wet soil and crushed leaves.
dietRestrictions: Avoid dry kibble, salted foods, herbicides, fertilizer salts, and pesticide-treated plants. Copper-based garden products can poison the bulb.

name: Ivysaur
route: Deep forest clearings, abandoned greenhouses, overgrown ruins, canyon shade, and humid preserve zones
habitat: Heavily vegetated areas with enough sunlight for the dorsal flower and enough cover for ambush feeding
behaviorTemperament: More territorial than Bulbasaur and less tolerant of handling. It may appear sleepy, then lash out when a person steps inside its root zone.
diet: Sunlight, water, berries, woody shoots, insects, small rodents, nutrient-rich soil, and decaying plant matter
activityPattern: Crepuscular and morning active. Roots itself during hot afternoons and during heavy bud growth.
bestTimeToFind: Morning after rain, at the edges of sunlit clearings where the bud can warm without drying out
observationDifficulty: Hard. It hides low among ferns and can remain rooted and motionless for hours.
conservationStatus: Protected and monitored due to high illegal capture risk
populationStatus: Uncommon in wild areas, mostly found in guarded territories
description: The adolescent stage of Bulbasaur. The bulb has opened into a heavy bud that drives the host toward heavier feeding, territorial behavior, and longer periods of rooted rest.
lore: Old ranger warnings say an Ivysaur clearing is quiet because everything small has already learned not to breathe there.
survivalUse: Can identify fertile soil, locate water, clear thorn brush with vines, and produce shade. Powder risk limits use in enclosed survival shelters.
handlingWarning: Never stand behind the head near the bud. Do not cut vines. Do not transport in a sealed car or tent.
encounterAdvice: Circle wide, stay upwind, and do not step on root trails. If the bud flexes open, retreat without turning your back.
firstAid: Use eye wash and soap-water decontamination. Give clean air for powder exposure. Seek medical help for weakness, wheezing, severe rash, or confusion.
companionSuitability: Fair with expert handlers, poor for casual homes
fieldNote: A healthy Ivysaur smells green and damp. A sour-sweet smell means the bud is sick, stressed, or about to vent chemicals.
originStory: Ivysaur likely represents the point where the ancient plant parasite learned to steer its host instead of simply riding it. Some wild specimens act less like animals and more like walking gardens defending a buried root network.

evolutionLine.name: Ivysaur
evolutionLine.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

mainLine.name: Ivysaur
mainLine.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

variants.method: Final stage only, special catalyst or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Venusaur and Gigantamax Venusaur are special battle forms, not normal field evolution
target.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

domesticationStatus: Captive-trained only under advanced grass-poison license
prey: Large insects, small mammals, berries, vines, tender bark, and fallen fruit
temperamentTriggers: Being forced to move while rooted
territoryRange: 1 to 4 square miles around water, shade, and sun access
lifeCycle: Evolves from Bulbasaur at level 16. Evolves into Venusaur at level 32 after the dorsal bud reaches full flowering mass.
containmentRequirements: Large humid outdoor pen with filtered sunlight, root barriers, air movement, washable surfaces, and emergency spore extraction.
ecologicalRole: Controls insects and small herbivores, spreads seeds, recycles leaf litter, and creates shaded microhabitats
diseaseParasiteRisk: High. Flower mites, bud rot, fungal bloom, spore allergy, and soil bacteria may affect handlers and the Pokemon.
reproductionNesting: Not normally the primary breeding stage. May guard nests made by Venusaur or defend juvenile Bulbasaur clusters.
predators: Charmeleon, Fearow, Arbok, large fire-type predators, and humans seeking rare starter lines
emergencyResponse: Evacuate downwind personnel, ventilate the area, rinse exposed skin and eyes, and treat respiratory symptoms as urgent.
legalStatus: Restricted due to toxic powder and invasive root risk
stressSicknessSigns: Standing still too long in shade
lifespan: Wild: 3 to 7 years. Managed care: 10 to 18 years if evolution is delayed responsibly.
socialStructure: Usually solitary or loosely attached to a Bulbasaur family territory. Avoids other Ivysaur unless breeding or competing for clearings.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Elemental output is vine force, sleep powder, poison powder, and solar growth.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Spore and pollen hazard about 15 to 40 ft in still air, farther with wind
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Low to moderate, relies on bracing
fieldCapabilities.balance: Stable but top-heavy when the bud is wet

trainingRisk: Vine crushing, sleep powder, poison powder, bud strain, and handler sensitization to spores
physiology: The plant partner has expanded deeper into the host, drawing more sugars, minerals, and nerve input. The bud can store powders, toxins, and growth chemicals, making Ivysaur more hazardous than its smaller form.

keyFacts.diet: Photosynthetic omnivore with higher protein demand
keyFacts.habitat: Humid forest clearings and overgrown ruins
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 3 to 7 years. Managed: 10 to 18 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary, sometimes one guardian with juveniles

domesticationDifficulty: High. The plant partner resists restraint and can poison enclosed air.
physicalCharacteristics: Larger quadruped with stronger legs, heavier shoulders, pointed ears, red eyes, and a large unopened flower bud on its back. Vines are thicker and more muscular than Bulbasaur vines.
dietRestrictions: Avoid dry enclosure conditions, lawn chemicals, animal feed with salt, and forced high-protein diets that outpace bud development.

name: Venusaur
route: Ancient forest basins, protected botanical reserves, old battlefields reclaimed by vegetation, and isolated sunlit groves
habitat: Large humid clearings with deep soil, stable water, and enough sunlight to support a fully flowering dorsal plant
classification: Seed Pokemon / Mature Symbiotic Flower Beast
behaviorTemperament: Calm until territory or young are threatened. Venusaur prefer stillness, using scent, pollen, and vine pressure before physical pursuit. A threatened adult can turn a clearing into a chemical hazard.
diet: Sunlight, large volumes of water, mineral-rich soil, fruits, woody plants, insects, and occasional carrion composted into soil around its resting site
activityPattern: Mostly diurnal and sedentary. Most movement occurs at dawn, dusk, or before storms.
bestTimeToFind: From a distance at sunrise when the flower opens for light and dew
observationDifficulty: Moderate to hard. Large body is visible, but old individuals resemble a mound of vegetation until they move.
populationStatus: Rare, usually one dominant adult per large territory
description: The fully developed Bulbasaur line. A Venusaur is less mobile than its younger stages but far more dangerous because the flower can release dense pollen, spores, and chemical signals over a wide area.
lore: Some villages built cemeteries around Venusaur groves because the flowers bloomed brightest over old soil and the adults kept scavengers away.
survivalUse: Can create shade, reveal water tables, stabilize damaged soil, deter predators, and support pollinators. Its presence can also make an area unsafe for unmasked humans.
handlingWarning: Never enter a Venusaur grove without respiratory protection and permission from the animal. Do not cut flowers or leaves.
encounterAdvice: Observe from high ground and upwind. If the flower turns toward you before the head does, leave immediately and quietly.
firstAid: For pollen inhalation, move to clean air and seek medical care. For vine crush injuries, immobilize limb and treat for shock. For sap exposure, wash thoroughly.
companionSuitability: Poor for private companionship, excellent as a sanctuary guardian when respected
fieldNote: Silence in a Venusaur grove is not peace. It often means the pollen has already driven smaller animals away.
originStory: The grim origin is a symbiosis that became inheritance. What began as a seed feeding from a host eventually became a body that could not live without the animal beneath it. A Venusaur is less a beast with a flower than a mobile grave-garden that learned loyalty.

evolutionLine.name: Venusaur
evolutionLine.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

mainLine.name: Venusaur
mainLine.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

variants.method: Special catalyst, battle phenomenon, or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Venusaur; Gigantamax Venusaur
target.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

prey: Insects, plant material, fruits, small animals caught by vines, and nutrient compost
territoryRange: 5 to 20 square miles depending on water and sunlight
lifeCycle: Final standard form of the Bulbasaur line after Ivysaur reaches level 32. May access Mega or Gigantamax forms only under special conditions.
containmentRequirements: A preserve, not a cage. Requires deep soil, root barriers, drainage, pollinator exclusion zones, spore monitoring, and emergency evacuation lanes.
ecologicalRole: Keystone shade maker, soil builder, pollinator signal hub, seed bank protector, and invasive plant suppressor
diseaseParasiteRisk: High for allergens, fungal blooms, mites, contaminated pollen, and secondary infections after vine injury
reproductionNesting: Females lay guarded eggs in composted plant beds. Adults shade and chemically defend the nesting area until hatchlings disperse.
predators: Very few natural predators. Charizard, Arcanine packs, severe drought, disease, and humans are the main threats.
emergencyResponse: Treat pollen clouds as hazardous atmosphere. Move upwind, rinse exposed areas, monitor breathing, and call trained grass-poison response handlers.
legalStatus: Restricted keystone Pokemon. Capture or transport normally requires environmental authority approval.
lifespan: Wild: 7 to 10 years in high-conflict regions. Managed sanctuary: 30 years or more.
socialStructure: Solitary matriarch or patriarch with tolerated young and lower-stage relatives on the edge of the territory

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Elemental output is heavy pollen, vines, seed discharge, and solar-stored growth energy.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Pollen and spore influence can affect a whole grove, often 50 to 150 ft or more with wind
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Slow burst, but vine reach is fast at close range
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Low body dodge, high vine interception
fieldCapabilities.balance: Extremely stable due to mass and low stance

trainingRisk: Pollen poisoning, vine crushing, sleep powder, territorial aggression, and enclosure overgrowth
physiology: The plant and animal portions are deeply integrated. The flower produces pollen, growth hormones, and chemical deterrents. Thick vascular roots inside the body draw water and minerals while the host heart supports both systems.

keyFacts.habitat: Ancient forest clearings and botanical preserves
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 7 to 10 years. Sanctuary: 30 years or more.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult with tolerated young nearby

domesticationDifficulty: Extreme. Requires land management, botanical hazard plans, and veterinary-botanical care.
physicalCharacteristics: Massive quadruped with thick legs, broad head, heavy body, large leaves, and a pink flowering crown. The flower may track sunlight or face threats independently of the head.
dietRestrictions: Avoid drought, concrete confinement, road salt, herbicides, heavy metals, and forced movement during flowering season.

name: Charmander
route: Dry foothills, volcanic scrub, sun-warmed caves, rocky badlands, and old charcoal pits
habitat: Warm rocky shelters with dry bedding, low humidity, airflow, and access to small prey
behaviorTemperament: Social, loyal, and curious when raised young. Wild Charmander are nervous pack juveniles that warn with tail flicks, hisses, and short flame bursts.
diet: Insects, small rodents, lizards, eggs, berries, charred plant matter, and high-energy protein meals
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular. Basks in morning sun and shelters during cold rain.
bestTimeToFind: Warm mornings near cave mouths, ash beds, or sunlit rocks with small claw marks
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Tail flame is visible at night but juveniles hide quickly.
populationStatus: Uncommon and declining near human development due to illegal capture and fire-control conflicts
description: A small fire-bearing reptilian Pokemon whose tail flame reflects health, stress, and stored fuel. Cute appearance should not hide the burn risk.
lore: Travelers once followed Charmander lights through badlands, but old stories warn that some lights led to dens where hungry adults waited.
survivalUse: Can provide heat, ignite emergency fire, deter small predators, and signal at night. Must never be used inside low-ventilation shelter.
handlingWarning: Never cover the tail flame with a blanket or water. Never carry near fuel, dry grass, tents, or aerosol cans.
encounterAdvice: Approach from the front at low height. Offer space and avoid water spray. If the tail flame flares white-blue, retreat.
firstAid: Cool burns for 20 minutes with clean water. Do not apply grease. For smoke inhalation, move to clean air and seek care. Tail flame collapse is veterinary emergency.
companionSuitability: Good for careful handlers with fire-safe housing
equipment: Water for humans, not for tail flame
fieldNote: A steady orange tail flame usually means calm health. A sputtering flame means illness, exhaustion, or wet conditions. A sudden flare means fear.
originStory: The fire line likely began in volcanic cave reptiles that survived cold nights by fermenting plant oils into flammable vapor. The first stable tail-flame mutations made them visible to predators, so only the boldest and most social survived.

evolutionLine.name: Charmander
evolutionLine.note: Juvenile fire-bearer. Tail flame is a health indicator and ignition source.

evolutionLine.name: Charmeleon
evolutionLine.note: Aggressive adolescent predator. Highest handler-conflict stage of the line.

evolutionLine.name: Charizard
evolutionLine.note: Aerial apex predator. Roosts require wildfire and aviation-style safety planning.

mainLine.name: Charmander
mainLine.note: Juvenile fire-bearer. Tail flame is a health indicator and ignition source.

mainLine.name: Charmeleon
mainLine.note: Aggressive adolescent predator. Highest handler-conflict stage of the line.

mainLine.name: Charizard
mainLine.note: Aerial apex predator. Roosts require wildfire and aviation-style safety planning.

target.name: Mega Charizard X
target.note: Temporary form with altered heat chemistry and more draconic combat behavior.

target.name: Mega Charizard Y
target.note: Temporary form with intensified aerial heat output and solar response.

target.name: Gigantamax Charizard
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat flame output as disaster-scale hazard.

prey: Insects, small mammals, carrion scraps, eggs, and roasted plant matter
territoryRange: Usually 1 to 3 square miles around den, water, basking rocks, and hunting lanes
lifeCycle: Hatches with a weak tail flame, grows by feeding and heat regulation, evolves into Charmeleon at level 16.
containmentRequirements: Fireproof den, ceramic or stone flooring, spark screen, dry bedding away from flame, ventilation, and Class ABC fire extinguisher nearby
ecologicalRole: Controls small pests, clears dry brush with low flame, and recycles carrion in arid habitats
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Smoke irritation, scale mites, fuel-gland infection, and respiratory illness in damp housing are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are kept in warm ash nests or geothermal pockets. Adults or dominant pack members guard eggs from damp and predators.
predators: Fearow, larger water-types, Arbok, humans, and stronger fire Pokemon competing for dens
emergencyResponse: For uncontrolled flame, remove fuel sources, guide Charmander to bare ground, and cool nearby burns with clean water without soaking the tail flame.
legalStatus: Fire-type handling permit recommended or required in many settled areas
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years before evolution or predation. Managed care: 12 to 20 years if kept healthy.
socialStructure: Pack juveniles under a dominant adult or older Charmeleon. Lone juveniles are more fearful and more likely to panic-burn.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is juvenile but capable of serious burns.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame and heat hazard about 3 to 10 ft, farther if dry grass ignites
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast short run about 12 to 18 mph
fieldCapabilities.balance: Tail improves balance but becomes liability in brush

trainingRisk: Accidental burns, panic ignition, smoke inhalation, fuel-gland strain, and tail injury
physiology: Charmander appears to generate flame using volatile biological fuel, oxygen intake, and a heat-resistant tail structure. The tail flame is not decorative. If it weakens severely, the animal is in medical danger.

keyFacts.habitat: Dry rocky caves and warm badlands
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years. Managed: 12 to 20 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually small packs or guarded juveniles

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Strong bonding potential but constant fire safety management required.
physicalCharacteristics: Orange bipedal body, cream underside, blue eyes, blunt claws, long tail, and a persistent open flame at the tail tip. Skin is smooth, warm, and resistant to brief heat exposure.
dietRestrictions: Avoid wet bedding, low-calorie diets, alcohol vapors, aerosol sprays, and overfeeding fatty scraps that can foul its fuel glands.

name: Charmeleon
route: Dry canyons, lava-tube caves, burned pine slopes, abandoned mines, and isolated badland ridges
habitat: Hot dry terrain with high perches, hunting corridors, and den sites shielded from rain
behaviorTemperament: Proud, irritable, territorial, and easily provoked. It may seek fights to establish rank and may ignore a handler it once obeyed as a Charmander.
diet: Small mammals, birds, eggs, reptiles, carrion, charred plant matter, and high-protein managed feed
activityPattern: Late afternoon, evening, and night hunting with daytime basking
bestTimeToFind: At dusk near warm rocks, cave exits, and fresh scorch trails
observationDifficulty: Hard and dangerous. Charmeleon often detects observers first and may circle downwind.
populationStatus: Rare and usually solitary outside breeding season
description: An aggressive adolescent fire Pokemon with stronger claws, a cranial horn, and a more intense tail flame. This stage is the most likely in the line to injure handlers.
lore: Old mining towns sometimes reported a red lizard in the tunnels before cave fires. Survivors said it did not hunt them at first. It waited until the smoke made them stumble.
survivalUse: Strong heat source, predator deterrent, cauterization risk tool in last-resort survival, and camp defense. Use is dangerous and can escalate wildfire risk.
handlingWarning: Do not challenge eye contact, grab the tail, block the den exit, or approach while it is feeding.
encounterAdvice: Give it an exit path. Do not run unless already behind cover. Place rock, dirt, or water barrier between you and dry vegetation.
firstAid: Cool burns, control bleeding, irrigate bites, avoid sealing deep punctures, and seek urgent care for any Charmeleon wound.
companionSuitability: Poor to fair. Suitable only for experienced fire-type handlers.
fieldNote: If Charmeleon lowers its head and the tail flame narrows, it is choosing a target. Backing away slowly is safer than shouting.
originStory: Charmeleon is the line at its most unstable: the body is building toward flight while the brain is flooded with hunger, heat, and dominance signals. In real-world terms, this would be a dangerous adolescent predator with a flamethrower attached to its spine.

evolutionLine.name: Charmeleon
evolutionLine.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

mainLine.name: Charmeleon
mainLine.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

variants.method: Final stage only, special catalyst or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Charizard X; Mega Charizard Y; Gigantamax Charizard
target.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

prey: Rattata, Pidgey, small reptiles, eggs, rodents, and carrion
territoryRange: 3 to 10 square miles, larger in poor hunting territory
lifeCycle: Evolves from Charmander at level 16 and can evolve into Charizard at level 36 after major muscular and respiratory development.
containmentRequirements: Stone den with double gates, flameproof walls, heat venting, remote feeding chute, and reinforced observation shield
ecologicalRole: Mid-level predator that controls rodents and scavenges carrion but can start brush fires in dry seasons
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Bite infections, smoke exposure, scale parasites, and fuel-gland inflammation are common captive issues.
reproductionNesting: Usually not a stable nesting stage. Competes for mates and may scavenge around established Charizard territories.
predators: Charizard, larger water-types, rock-types, rival Charmeleon, and humans
emergencyResponse: If it ignites brush, prioritize evacuation and fuel breaks. For bites or burns, treat as contaminated trauma and seek emergency care.
legalStatus: Restricted fire predator. Transport in settled areas usually requires fire marshal or wildlife approval.
lifespan: Wild: 7 to 15 years. Managed care: 20 years or more with expert housing.
socialStructure: Mostly solitary. Temporary dominance interactions with packs or breeding groups are volatile.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is strong enough to ignite brush and cause deep burns.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame hazard commonly 10 to 25 ft, indirect wildfire risk much farther
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast ground burst about 20 to 25 mph

trainingRisk: Deep claw wounds, serious burns, biting, panic fire, and handler challenge behavior
physiology: The flame system intensifies after evolution. Internal fuel stores and heat-control tissues expand, raising burn output while also making the animal prone to agitation when overheated or underfed.

keyFacts.habitat: Dry canyons, lava tubes, burned slopes
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 7 to 15 years. Managed: 20 years or more.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary except brief breeding contact

domesticationDifficulty: High due to dominance behavior and fire risk
physicalCharacteristics: Red-orange biped with sharper claws, narrow eyes, stronger jaws, head crest or horn, long tail, and hotter tail flame. Developing shoulder and back musculature foreshadows Charizard wings.
dietRestrictions: Avoid low-protein diets, sugary human food, damp bedding, enclosed smoke exposure, and forced fasting during growth spurts.

name: Charizard
route: Mountain nests, volcanic ridges, high cliffs, old fortress ruins, and remote burned valleys
habitat: High warm terrain with strong updrafts, cave roosts, open flight lanes, and limited human access
behaviorTemperament: Independent, proud, and highly territorial. It may ignore small prey while actively challenging strong creatures or intruders near its roost.
diet: Large mammals, birds, fish, carrion, high-fat prey, charred vegetation, and managed high-protein feed
activityPattern: Diurnal flight and dusk hunting. Roosts during storms and extreme cold.
bestTimeToFind: From far away at sunrise near cliff roosts when warm air begins rising
observationDifficulty: High. Easy to spot in flight, hard to survive if too close to a nesting adult.
conservationStatus: Strictly protected apex species with public safety restrictions
populationStatus: Rare. Stable only in remote protected ranges.
description: A large flying fire predator capable of sustained flight, intense flame projection, and territorial attacks. In survival terms, Charizard is not a campfire helper. It is an aerial apex hazard.
lore: Ancient settlements painted Charizard silhouettes over gates to warn travelers which valleys had no law after sunset.
survivalUse: Can provide aerial evacuation, predator deterrence, signal fire, and heat in extreme emergencies. The risk of attracting or causing disaster is severe.
handlingWarning: Never approach a roost, egg, injured wing, or feeding adult. Do not shine lights into its eyes at night.
encounterAdvice: Do not stand in open ground. Move under rock cover, stay low, avoid smoke plumes, and leave the territory without running toward cliffs.
firstAid: For burns, cool immediately and seek emergency care. For bite or claw wounds, control bleeding and treat as contaminated trauma. For smoke exposure, move to clean air.
companionSuitability: Poor for homes. Possible working partnership only with elite handlers and protected range access.
resistances: Ground immunity in game type logic
fieldNote: If a Charizard circles without attacking, it may be measuring wind, escape paths, and whether you are worth the energy.
originStory: The Charizard line appears to have evolved from heat-cave predators that traded camouflage for fire. Its darkest adaptation is psychological: a healthy adult often refuses weak prey, seeking stronger opponents because combat itself selects mates and territory.

evolutionLine.name: Charizard
evolutionLine.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

mainLine.name: Charizard
mainLine.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

variants.method: Special catalyst, battle phenomenon, or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Charizard X; Mega Charizard Y; Gigantamax Charizard
target.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

domesticationStatus: Not domesticated. Historical riding and military use required heavy training and high casualty risk.
prey: Large mammals, fish, birds, smaller Pokemon, livestock, and carrion
territoryRange: 20 to 100 square miles depending on prey, cliffs, and thermal lift
lifeCycle: Final standard form of the Charmander line after Charmeleon reaches level 36. May access Mega or Gigantamax forms under rare special conditions.
containmentRequirements: A managed flight range with stone roosts, firebreaks, remote feeding systems, veterinary restraint bay, and aviation hazard coordination
ecologicalRole: Apex aerial predator, carrion remover, fire-cycle driver in adapted scrublands, and population control for large herbivores
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Burn wounds, respiratory soot, wing mites, salmonella-like reptile bacteria, and carrion-borne pathogens may affect handlers.
reproductionNesting: Nests on cliffs, lava-warmed stone, or high ruins. Eggs require warmth, ventilation, and long guarding periods.
predators: Few natural predators. Rival Charizard, large rock-types, severe wing injury, disease, and humans are primary threats.
emergencyResponse: Treat as wildfire plus large predator incident. Evacuate downwind, notify fire and wildlife teams, and do not attempt capture without aerial support.
legalStatus: Restricted apex fire-flying Pokemon. Private ownership usually prohibited or heavily regulated.
lifespan: Wild: 15 to 30 years. Managed sanctuary: 40 years or more.
socialStructure: Usually solitary. May form temporary breeding pair or loose dominance network around rich territory.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is extreme and capable of structural ignition.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame hazard 50 ft or more, smoke and wildfire hazard far beyond line of sight
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast ground lunge and high-speed takeoff
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High in air, moderate on ground
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Wing-assisted launch from cliffs or open ground
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent in air, tail flame affects tight ground turns

trainingRisk: Fatal burns, aerial drops, crushing bites, wing-strike trauma, wildfire, and dominance attacks
physiology: Charizard uses powerful lungs, internal fuel chemistry, and a heat-resistant throat to project flame. Its wings demand high calories, and injured adults can become desperate near livestock, camps, and roads.

keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 15 to 30 years. Sanctuary: 40 years or more.

domesticationDifficulty: Extreme. Requires aviation-scale space, fire control, and specialist handlers.
physicalCharacteristics: Orange dragon-like body, cream underside, broad wings, horned head, heavy tail flame, powerful jaws, large claws, and heat-hardened hide. Wing membranes are vulnerable to tears despite the animal strength.
dietRestrictions: Avoid low-calorie feeding, confinement without flight, wet cold roosts, and overuse of flame in enclosed areas.

name: Squirtle
route: Freshwater ponds, marsh edges, slow rivers, seaweed beaches, rain ditches, and protected coastal coves
habitat: Clean freshwater or brackish water with basking stones, sandy banks, submerged plants, and escape tunnels
classification: Tiny Turtle Pokemon / Juvenile Water Chelonian
behaviorTemperament: Curious, social, and playful near water. Wild Squirtle retreat quickly, then spray from cover. Cornered individuals snap at fingers and eyes.
diet: Aquatic insects, algae, small fish, mollusks, berries, water plants, mosquito larvae, and prepared aquatic feed
activityPattern: Diurnal with strong dawn and afternoon basking. More active during warm rain.
bestTimeToFind: Morning on basking rocks, muddy banks, or shallow coves after rain
observationDifficulty: Easy to moderate. Tracks and shell slides are common, but it dives quickly.
populationStatus: Stable in protected waters, declining in polluted urban canals
description: A small aquatic turtle Pokemon with a hardening shell and pressurized water spray. Usually manageable, but mishandling can cause bites, water-jet injuries, and drowning hazards.
lore: Old coastal families considered Squirtle a lucky find until children learned that a scared one could blind a person for minutes with one panicked spray.
survivalUse: Can find water, catch small aquatic food, spray fires at close range, clean mud from tools, and warn of polluted water by refusing to enter.
handlingWarning: Support the shell with both hands. Do not pick up by the tail or limbs. Keep face away from mouth and spray angle.
encounterAdvice: Approach slowly from land, offer an escape route to water, and avoid grabbing a basking group. A calm Squirtle may approach shiny gear.
firstAid: Clean bites with soap and water. Rinse eyes after spray impact. Keep an overheated Squirtle in shallow clean water and shade.
companionSuitability: Good for beginners who respect water, shell, and hygiene needs
fieldNote: A Squirtle that stops basking and stares at water may have sensed vibration before humans hear anything.
originStory: Squirtle likely descended from small shoreline turtles that survived predation by weaponizing stored water. The shell became more than armor: it became a pressure vessel.

evolutionLine.name: Squirtle
evolutionLine.note: Juvenile aquatic turtle. Water spray and bite risk increase when cornered.

evolutionLine.name: Wartortle
evolutionLine.note: Stronger and more territorial. Tail and ears help water maneuvering.

evolutionLine.name: Blastoise
evolutionLine.note: Heavy armored adult with cannon-like water projection. Treat as pressure hazard.

mainLine.name: Squirtle
mainLine.note: Juvenile aquatic turtle. Water spray and bite risk increase when cornered.

mainLine.name: Wartortle
mainLine.note: Stronger and more territorial. Tail and ears help water maneuvering.

mainLine.name: Blastoise
mainLine.note: Heavy armored adult with cannon-like water projection. Treat as pressure hazard.

target.name: Mega Blastoise
target.note: Temporary form with concentrated cannon pressure. Risk shifts from drowning to impact trauma.

target.name: Gigantamax Blastoise
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Water projection can damage structures and flood confined spaces.

prey: Mosquito larvae, aquatic insects, small fish, algae, soft plants, and mollusks
territoryRange: A pond, marsh edge, or 0.5 to 2 miles of riverbank
lifeCycle: Hatches from soft-shelled eggs near water, grows through juvenile aquatic stages, evolves into Wartortle at level 16.
containmentRequirements: Clean filtered water, basking area, dry dock, smooth rocks, secure lid, shell-safe humidity, and no small swallowable plastic
ecologicalRole: Mosquito control, algae grazing, small aquatic predator, and indicator of clean shallow water
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Salmonella-like bacteria, shell rot, parasites, and contaminated water exposure are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Females bury eggs in warm sand or soft bank soil above flood level. Hatchlings run to water and hide in vegetation.
predators: Fearow, Arbok, Golbat, large fish, snapping predators, humans, and polluted waterways
emergencyResponse: For water-jet eye injury, rinse and seek care if pain persists. For bites, wash thoroughly. For escaped Squirtle, search drains, tubs, ponds, and damp shade.
legalStatus: Protected aquatic starter. Transport may require clean-water documentation.
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years. Managed care: 25 to 40 years with clean water and shell care.
socialStructure: Small basking groups and juvenile creches near safe coves. Adults become more independent after evolution.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Water output is juvenile but pressurized enough to injure eyes and knock loose objects away.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Spray range about 15 to 25 ft depending on hydration and stress
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Short swim burst around 15 to 20 mph, slower on land
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Fast dive reflex, moderate land dodge
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Low, about 1 to 2 ft from bank edge
fieldCapabilities.balance: Good in water, clumsy on slick hard floors

trainingRisk: Biting, slippery escape, water spray to eyes, and shell stress from dropping
physiology: Squirtle can breathe air but stores and expels water with unusual pressure through muscular body compression. Its shell protects organs while also helping control buoyancy and hydrodynamic movement.

keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years. Managed: 25 to 40 years.

domesticationDifficulty: Low to moderate, mostly water quality and shell health
physicalCharacteristics: Blue skin, curled tail, cream belly plate, brown shell, large eyes, webbed feet, and a short beak-like mouth. The shell is softer in hatchlings and hardens with age.
dietRestrictions: Avoid chlorinated pools, salty snacks, dirty aquarium water, bread diets, and dry heat without soaking access.

name: Pikachu
route: Forest edges, grassy routes, storm-prone woodland
habitat: Woodlands, open grass, abandoned power areas, and regions with frequent electrical activity.
behaviorTemperament: Alert, social, curious, and skittish when surprised.
diet: Berries, fruit, seeds, and small field foods.
activityPattern: Mostly diurnal, but activity increases before storms.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning, late afternoon, or storm weather.
description: A small electric creature known for storing electrical charge in its cheek sacs.
lore: Pikachu are alert, social, and highly reactive when frightened.
survivalUse: Can warn of storms and electrical disturbance.
handlingWarning: Do not grab barehanded. Avoid cheeks and tail.
encounterAdvice: Keep distance, stay calm, avoid sudden movement.
firstAid: Treat shock as serious and seek medical help.
fieldNote: If injured, keep calm, dry, and away from metal tools.
originStory: Early ranger accounts place the first confirmed Pikachu encounter near storm-charged caves.

evolutionLine.name: Pichu
evolutionLine.note: Small and unstable. Electrical output is harder to control.

evolutionLine.name: Pikachu
evolutionLine.note: Primary field form. Strong companion potential with trained handlers.

evolutionLine.name: Raichu
evolutionLine.note: Standard final evolution with stronger electrical output.

mainLine.name: Pichu
mainLine.note: Small and unstable. Electrical output is harder to control.

mainLine.name: Pikachu
mainLine.note: Primary field form. Strong companion potential with trained handlers.

mainLine.name: Raichu
mainLine.note: Standard final evolution with stronger electrical output.

target.name: Alolan Raichu
target.note: Regional branch connected to Alolan conditions.

prey: Small insects, berries, seeds, fruit, and field scraps
territoryRange: Small forest-edge range or colony area
lifeCycle: Born as Pichu, matures into Pikachu, may evolve into Raichu with Thunder Stone.
containmentRequirements: Grounded enclosure, rubberized flooring, insulated tools.
ecologicalRole: Controls small insects and spreads seeds
reproductionNesting: Nests in dry burrows, hollow logs, cave pockets, or root systems.
predators: Large ground predators and aerial hunters
emergencyResponse: Avoid metal and standing water during discharge.
legalStatus: Ownership may require electric-type handling certification
lifespan: Wild: 6 to 8 years. Managed care: 10 to 15 years.
socialStructure: Tight-knit colonies with shared warning signals
physiology: Stores electrical energy in specialized cheek sacs.

keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 6 to 8 years. Managed care: 10 to 15 years
keyFacts.groupSize: Small groups to colonies of up to 30

domesticationDifficulty: High for wild adults, moderate if raised young
physicalCharacteristics: Small yellow rodent-like Pokemon with red cheek sacs and a lightning-shaped tail.
dietRestrictions: Avoid salty processed foods and exposed wiring.
